```md app/README.md
# Otovo App

This is the React Native-based Otovo app for Android and iOS.
The main functionality of the app is to enable an Otovo customer to monitor the production of their solar installation,
but we're planning to add new features very soon.

**We've recently completely revamped the development and deployment experience for the App,
so consider this document to be a "living one" for some time :)**

## Expo

[Expo](https://docs.expo.dev/) is a framework and a platform for universal React applications. It supplies a set of tools and services that
simplify the development and testing of React Native apps, offering/giving the possibility to run the code on your phone easily.
Besides that, Expo provides a more robust and developer-friendly workflow.

There are two tools that you need to develop apps with Expo: a command line app called Expo CLI to initialize and serve your project
and a mobile client app called Expo Go to open it on iOS and Android. You can also open the app in an iOS simulator or an Android emulator.

**Important**: You don't need to own a MacBook to run and build an iOS app with Expo. For your development machine, Windows, Linux, and macOS are all supported.

## Getting started

- Install Expo Go app for Android or iOS
- Install project dependencies by running:

   ```bash
   cd app && expo install
   ```
(Expo's CLI is's [already bundled in the project](https://blog.expo.dev/the-new-expo-cli-f4250d8e3421).)

## Developing

### Setting up a development build


Since we are using dependencies with native code, we cannot use Expo Go. In order to set up our development environment, [we need to create a development build](https://docs.expo.dev/develop/development-builds/introduction/).

```bash
yarn build:dev # For physical devices and Android emulator
yarn build:dev-simulator # for iOS simulator

```
ŌÅ▒’ĖÅ This process takes approximately ~10 minutes.

We only need to create a new build every time we update dependencies and change app versions. You use existing builds in [this list](https://expo.dev/accounts/otovo/projects/Otovo/development-builds).

### Running the server

- Start the development server by running `yarn start`.
  - If you want to run with the production API you can run `yarn start:preview`. NB! We currently can't run the app on physical devices with a local dev server.
- ­¤ŹÄ On your iPhone or iPad, open the default Apple "Camera" app and scan the QR code you see in the terminal or in Expo Dev Tools.
- ­¤ż¢ On your Android device, press "Scan QR Code" on the "Projects" tab of the Expo Go app and scan the QR code you see in the terminal or in Expo Dev Tools.
- You can also use the [iOS Simulator](https://docs.expo.dev/workflow/ios-simulator/) or the [Android Studio Emulator](https://docs.expo.dev/workflow/android-studio-emulator/) to run the app. You can enter `a` for android or `i` of ios after the server has starter to use these emulators instead.
- If you want to open a debugger, press `j`, or shake your device to open the dev menu.


## Releasing new App versions for internal distrubtion or to app stores

The Otovo App is setup for automatic release to both App Store and Google Play Store. We are currently using [EAS Build](https://docs.expo.io/build/introduction/) for our continuous deployment. You need to have EAS CLI installed and to be logged in with your [Expo account](https://expo.dev). See step 1 and 2 [here](https://docs.expo.dev/build/setup/#1-install-the-latest-eas-cli).

> **Note**
> Ask a senior developer to get invited to Expo. They have access to the account owner credentials stored in [the Otovo Pass repo](https://github.com/otovo/pass) and can invite you.

There are 3 different ways of deploying an Expo app, and it's important to know the difference:

#### Internal Distribution

`yarn build:preview`

Useful for sharing the test build with a wider audience inside Otovo. See more info [here](https://docs.expo.dev/build/internal-distribution/), especially the section about app signing on iOS.
#### Release to app stores:
`yarn build:release <major|minor|patch>`

This command will build the binaries, bump the app version in the package.json and submit the newly created version to [App Store Connect](https://appstoreconnect.apple.com/apps) and [Google Play Console](https://play.google.com/console/). From there you can use Apple TestFlight or Google Internal Testing, update the release notes and release the new version to customers.
#### **OTA (Over The Air) Updates**
`yarn update:<dev|dev-simulator|preview|production>`

Sometimes, we need to patch something in our binaries. However, submitting new builds takes a lot of time. In these cases we can send an over-the-air update with `eas update`.

Merge the hotfix to master and run `yarn update:prod` with main checked out. The update will be sent to all apps with the same app version. When users open their app, the app will check for updates and attempt to download the new changes. As these changes will not be applied immediately, the user will have to force close the app a couple of times before they are applied. The UX here is therefore not great -- In a later version we can add a loading indicator if the app detects a new change. This is now possible with [the new Updates API](https://docs.expo.dev/versions/latest/sdk/updates/#useupdates).


## Tests

This project is set up to use [jest](https://facebook.github.io/jest/) for
tests. You can configure whatever testing strategy you like, but jest works out
of the box. Create test files in directories called `__tests__` to have the
files loaded by jest.

See the [the template
project](https://github.com/react-community/create-react-native-app/tree/master/react-native-scripts/template/__tests__)
for an example test. The [jest
documentation](https://facebook.github.io/jest/docs/getting-started.html) is
also a wonderful resource, as is the [React Native testing
tutorial](https://facebook.github.io/jest/docs/tutorial-react-native.html).

```bash
yarn test
```

Runs the [jest](https://github.com/facebook/jest) test runner on your tests.

## Style guide

JavaScript is formatted using Prettier. ESlint is set up to respect Prettier's
rules. See `.eslintrc.json` for our custom options. We recommend adding a
shortcut to running Prettier in the current file to your IDE. Depending on your
setup this might have to be done manually (Webstorm), or you might find a
package (e.g. [prettier-atom](https://atom.io/packages/prettier-atom) if you
are using Atom)

## Translations

We use [PhraseApp](https://app.phrase.com) for translations. Ask one of the developers if you need access.

App translation workflow:

- Every app screen has an auxiliary `messages.js` file. The first step then is to add a new key to `messages` object. Give the key a meaningful name for future code readability. The value of this key should be an object itself, consisting of and ID and default message string (needed if the key has not been translated yet).
- In `<ScreenName>Screen` file, import `messages` object, if it has not been imported yet:
  `import { messages as m } from './messages';`,
- Also, import translation function:
   `import { t } from '../../lib/i18n/i18n';`
- To include a localized string, call the above function:
  `t(m.<theNewTranslationKey>)`
- Once all the strings are included, run the following from the project root:
  `node extract_i18n.js`. This extracts and saves keys from your `messages.js`-files to `locale/en-GB.json`
- Run `yarn phrase-push` to submit newly created keys to Phrase.
- Translate the uploaded keys. You can find the latest upload [here](https://app.phrase.com/accounts/otovo/projects/app/uploads).
- In `App/lib/i18n/config.js` file, import `intl/locale-data/jsonp/<Language>` and follow the same structure inside `supportedLocales` variable for this new language.
- In `App/lib/i18n/i18n.js` file, import the new `NAMESPACE` in which you have created new keys and messages. Do not forget to write this new `NAMESPACE` inside the `namespaces` array.
- In `App/lib/moment.js` file, follow the same structure for `moment.updateLocale()` for the new language. You can follow [these](https://github.com/moment/moment/tree/develop/locale) similar structures and make the correct adjustments for each language.
- Once the app build is triggered, it will automatically pull the translations from Phrase and the content will be translated.

It is highly recommended to switch the language on your development device or a simulator to check how the content is rendered in different languages :)

## Troubleshooting

### Networking

If you're unable to load your app on your phone due to a network timeout or a
refused connection, a good first step is to verify that your phone and computer
are on the same network and that they can reach each other. Create React Native
App needs access to ports 19000 and 19001 so ensure that your network and
firewall settings allow access from your device to your computer on both of
these ports.

### iOS Simulator won't open

If you're on a Mac, there are a few errors that users sometimes see when
attempting to `yarn run ios`:

- "non-zero exit code: 107"
- "You may need to install Xcode" but it is already installed
- and others

There are a few steps you may want to take to troubleshoot these kinds of errors:

1. Make sure Xcode is installed and open it to accept the license agreement if
   it prompts you. You can install it from the Mac App Store.

2. Open Xcode's Preferences, the Locations tab, and make sure that the `Command
   Line Tools` menu option is set to something. Sometimes when the CLI tools
   are first installed by Homebrew this option is left blank, which can prevent
   Apple utilities from finding the simulator. Make sure to re-run `yarn run
   ios` after doing so.

3. If that doesn't work, open the Simulator, and under the app menu select
   `Reset Contents and Settings...`. After that has finished, quit the
   Simulator, and re-run `yarn run ios`.
```

```markdown
## Development Setup

### 1. Create a Development Build

We use custom native code, so Expo Go isn't suitable. Instead, we need to create a development build:

```bash
yarn build:dev        # For physical devices and Android emulator
yarn build:dev-simulator  # For iOS simulator
```

ŌÅ▒’ĖÅ This process takes about 10 minutes.

Note: You only need to create a new build when updating dependencies or changing app versions. Find existing builds [here](https://expo.dev/accounts/otovo/projects/Otovo/development-builds).

### 2. Run the Development Server

Choose the appropriate command based on your target:

| Command | Use Case | API Connection | Expo DevTools |
|---------|----------|----------------|---------------|
| `yarn start` | Simulators only | Local Cloud | Included |
| `yarn start:preview` | Physical devices | Production Cloud | Included |
| `yarn start:prod` | Physical devices | Production Cloud | Not included |

### 3. Connect Your Device

- ­¤ŹÄ iOS: Open the Camera app and scan the QR code
- ­¤ż¢ Android: Use the Expo Go app to scan the QR code
- Simulators: Type `i` for iOS or `a` for Android in the terminal

### Tips

- Toggle between Expo Go and EAS builds: Press `s` after the QR code appears
- Open debugger: Press `j` or shake your device
- For detailed setup of iOS Simulator or Android Studio Emulator, check the [Expo documentation](https://docs.expo.dev/workflow/ios-simulator/)

For a comprehensive overview of different builds and how EAS and Expo Go work, watch [this video](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7J8LRpja9_o).
```

This revised version:

1. Uses clear, numbered steps for the setup process.
2. Presents the server run options in a table for easy comparison.
3. Simplifies the device connection instructions.
4. Moves less crucial information (like the video) to the end.
5. Uses more concise language throughout.

This structure should make it easier for developers to quickly find the information they need and avoid common mistakes.

```sh app/scripts/build.sh
    #!/bin/bash

    usage() {
      echo "Usage: $0 {dev|dev-simulator|preview|prod|release <major|minor|patch>}"
      exit 1
    }

    if [ -z "$1" ]; then
      usage
    fi

    # Handle versioning for release
    if [ "$1" == "release" ]; then
      if [ -z "$2" ]; then
        echo "Error: Version type (major, minor, patch) must be specified for release."
        usage
      fi
      npm version "$2"
    fi

    case "$1" in
      dev)
        eas build --profile development
        ;;
      dev-simulator)
        eas build --profile development-simulator --platform ios
        ;;
      preview)
        eas build --profile preview
        ;;
      prod)
        eas build --profile production
        ;;
      release)
        eas build --profile production --auto-submit
        ;;
      *)
        usage
        ;;
    esac
```

```sh app/scripts/cloud-pull.sh
#!/usr/bin/env bash
echo "== Pulling latest config from Cloud =="

if [[ -f .env ]]; then
  source .env
fi

if [[ -z "$CLOUD_CONFIG_API_TOKEN" ]]; then
  echo "Cloud: Missing env var CLOUD_CONFIG_API_TOKEN"
  exit 1
fi

CLOUD_CONFIG_API_URL="https://api.otovo.com/businessunits/businessunitconfig/"

RUNNING_DIR="$( cd "$( dirname "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}" )" >/dev/null 2>&1 && pwd )"
OUTPUT="$RUNNING_DIR/App/lib/config/cloudConfig.json"

echo "pulling $CLOUD_CONFIG_API_URL"
curl -X GET $CLOUD_CONFIG_API_URL -H "Authorization: Token $CLOUD_CONFIG_API_TOKEN" > $OUTPUT 

echo "Prettifying output..."
$PWD/node_modules/.bin/prettier --write $OUTPUT --parser json
echo "Done!"
```

```sh app/scripts/update.sh
#!/bin/bash

# Function to display usage
usage() {
  echo "Usage: $0 {development|simulator|preview|production}"
  exit 1
}

# Load environment variables from .env
if [ -f .env ]; then
  export $(grep -v '^#' .env | xargs)
fi

# Check if SENTRY_AUTH_TOKEN is set
if [ -z "$SENTRY_AUTH_TOKEN" ]; then
  echo "SENTRY_AUTH_TOKEN environment variable must be set."
  exit 1
fi

# Check if the argument is provided
if [ -z "$1" ]; then
  usage
fi

# Run the appropriate update command based on the argument
case "$1" in
  development)
    ENVIRONMENT=development eas update --branch development && yarn sentry:upload
    ;;
  simulator)
    ENVIRONMENT=development eas update --branch development-simulator --platform ios && yarn sentry:upload
    ;;
  preview)
    ENVIRONMENT=preview eas update --branch preview && yarn sentry:upload
    ;;
  production)
    ENVIRONMENT=production eas update --branch production && yarn sentry:upload
    ;;
  *)
    usage
    ;;
esac
```

Certainly! I'll add more details about the `build:dev` and `build:dev-simulator` commands and their relationship with EAS (Expo Application Services). Here's an updated version of that section:

```markdown
### 1. Create a Development Build

We use custom native code, so Expo Go isn't suitable. Instead, we need to create a development build using EAS:

```bash
yarn build:dev        # For physical devices and Android emulator
yarn build:dev-simulator  # For iOS simulator
```

These commands do the following:

- `yarn build:dev`:
  - Triggers an EAS build with the "development" profile
  - Builds for both iOS and Android platforms
  - Includes development-specific configurations
  - Allows running on physical devices and Android emulators

- `yarn build:dev-simulator`:
  - Triggers an EAS build with the "development-simulator" profile
  - Builds only for iOS platform
  - Optimized for running in the iOS simulator
  - Includes development-specific configurations

ŌÅ▒’ĖÅ Each build process takes about 10 minutes.

Note: You only need to create a new build when updating dependencies or changing app versions. Find existing builds [here](https://expo.dev/accounts/otovo/projects/Otovo/development-builds).

After building, EAS will provide you with a URL to download the app or install it directly on your device/simulator.
```

This explanation provides more context about what each build command does, how it relates to EAS, and what developers can expect from the process. It also clarifies the differences between the two development build options.

  const [loading, setLoading] = useState(false);
  const [errorMessage, setErrorMessage] = useState('');
  const config = getConfigByI18nLocale();
  const emailInputRef = React.useRef<ElementRef<typeof TextInput | null>>(null);
  const passwordInputRef =
    React.useRef<ElementRef<typeof TextInput | null>>(null);

  const doLogin = async () => {
    if (!validateEmail(email) || !password) {
      setErrorMessage(t(m.invalidCredentials));
      return;
    }

    setLoading(true);
    try {
      await authenticateUserWithPassword(email.trim().toLowerCase(), password);
      logEvent(
        'LOGIN_SUCCESS',
        {
          screenName: route.name,
          reason: `${email} used correct email and password`,
        },
        email,
      );
    } catch (e) {
      switch (e.message) {
        case ERROR_CODE.INVALID_CREDENTIALS:
          logEvent(
            'LOGIN_ERROR',
            {
              screenName: route.name,
              reason: `${email} - ${ERROR_CODE.INVALID_CREDENTIALS}`,
            },
            email,
          );
          setErrorMessage(t(m.invalidCredentials));
          break;
        default:
          logEvent(
            'LOGIN_ERROR',
            {
              screenName: route.name,
              reason: e.message || 'Unknown Error',
            },
            email,
          );
          setErrorMessage(e.message || t(commonMessages.unknownError));
          break;
      }
    }
    setLoading(false);
  };

  const handleChange = (type) => (change: string) => {
    if (errorMessage.length > 0) {
      setErrorMessage('');
    }
    switch (type) {
      case 'password':
        setPassword(change);
        break;
      case 'email':
        setEmail(change);
        break;
      default:
        break;
    }
  };

  return (
    <Page bg={sunset}>
      <LogoHeader variant="default" />
      <Text
        color={errorMessage ? 'red_70' : 'lilac_100'}
        fontWeight="medium"
        mx="7"
        mb="8"
        textAlign="center">
        {errorMessage || t(m.subheader)}
      </Text>
      <KeyboardAvoidingView
        flex={1}
        mx="8"
        behavior={Platform.OS === 'ios' ? 'padding' : undefined}
        keyboardVerticalOffset={50}
        enabled>
        <Box>
          <InputComponent
            value={email}
            placeholder={t(m.email)}
            returnKeyType="next"
            onChange={handleChange('email')}
            ref={emailInputRef}
            onSubmitEditing={() => {
              passwordInputRef.current?.focus();
            }}
          />
        </Box>
        <Box mt="4">
          <InputComponent
            value={password}
            placeholder={t(m.password)}
            returnKeyType="done"
            onChange={handleChange('password')}
            secure
            ref={passwordInputRef}
          />
          <Link
            textAlign="right"
            mt="5"
            fontWeight="medium"
            fontSize="2"
            openInAppBrowser
            handlePress={() => {
              logEvent(
                'LINK_CLICKED',
                {
                  screenName: route.name,
                  href: `${config.storefront_urls.production}/password/reset/`,
                  text: m.forgotPassword.defaultMessage,
                  reason: email,
                },
                email,
              );
            }}
            to={withUTM(
              `${config.storefront_urls.production}/password/reset/`,
              'PasswordLoginScreen',
            )}>
            {t(m.forgotPassword)}
          </Link>
        </Box>
        <Box mt="8">
          <Button onPress={doLogin} loading={loading} minWidth="100%">
            {t(m.logIn)}
          </Button>
        </Box>
      </KeyboardAvoidingView>

      <BuildInfo />

      <Banner
        onPress={() => {
          logEvent(
            'BOTTOM_BANNER_CLICK',
            {
              screenName: route.name,
              reason: `${email} navigated to OtpLoginScreen`,
              text: m.loginWithOneTimePassword.defaultMessage,
            },
            email,
          );
          navigation.goBack();
        }}>
        {t(m.loginWithOneTimePassword)}
      </Banner>
    </Page>
  );
};

export default PasswordLoginScreen;

import {
  Animated,
  Easing,
  StyleSheet,
  TextInput,
  type TextInputProps,
} from 'react-native';
import { size, theme } from '../lib/theme';

import OpenKeyboard from './Keyboard/OpenKeyboard';

const styles = StyleSheet.create({
  wrapperStyles: {
    backgroundColor: 'white',
    borderBottomWidth: 2,
    borderColor: 'white',
    borderLeftWidth: 2,
    borderRightWidth: 2,
    borderTopWidth: 2,
    width: '100%',
    position: 'relative',
  },
  inputStyles: {
    backgroundColor: 'transparent',
    borderBottomColor: 'transparent', // reset default styling
    borderLeftColor: 'transparent', // reset default styling
    borderRightColor: 'transparent', // reset default styling
    borderTopColor: 'transparent', // reset default styling
    color: theme.colors.grey_80,
    fontFamily: 'Poppins-Regular',
    fontSize: size(4),
    paddingHorizontal: size(6),
    paddingVertical: size(4),
  },
  iconButtonStyles: {
    position: 'absolute',
    right: 20,
    top: '50%',
    transform: [{ translateY: -18 }],
    zIndex: 100,
  },
});

type Props = TextInputProps & {
  onChange: (change: string) => mixed,
  secure?: boolean,
  value: string,
  error?: boolean,
  style?: Object,
};

const InputComponent = forwardRef<ElementRef<typeof TextInput>, Props>(
  (
    {
      autoCapitalize = 'none',
      keyboardType = 'default',
      maxLength = 64,
      onChange,
      placeholder,
      returnKeyType = 'default',
      secure = false,
      value,
      error,
      style,
      ...restProps
    }: Props,
    ref,
  ) => {
    const [cubicAnimation] = useState(new Animated.Value(0));
    const [linearAnimation] = useState(new Animated.Value(0));

    const handleFocus = (shouldFocus: boolean) => {
      Animated.parallel([
        Animated.timing(cubicAnimation, {
          toValue: shouldFocus ? 1 : 0,
          duration: 200,
          easing: shouldFocus ? Easing.cubic : Easing.out(Easing.cubic),
          useNativeDriver: false,
        }),

        Animated.timing(linearAnimation, {
          toValue: shouldFocus ? 1 : 0,
          duration: 200,
          easing: Easing.linear,
          useNativeDriver: false,
        }),
      ]).start();
    };

    if (!ref) {
      throw new Error('InputComponent requires a ref');
    }

    const borderColor = linearAnimation.interpolate({
      inputRange: [0, 1],
      outputRange: [
        'white',
        error ? theme.colors.scarlet : theme.colors.digital,
      ],
    });

    const radii = cubicAnimation.interpolate({
      inputRange: [0, 1],
      outputRange: [size(2), size(10)],
    });

    return (
      <Animated.View
        style={[
          styles.wrapperStyles,
          style,
          {
            borderColor,
            borderTopLeftRadius: radii,
            borderTopRightRadius: radii,
            borderBottomLeftRadius: radii,
            borderBottomRightRadius: radii,
          },
        ]}>
        <TextInput
          ref={ref}
          placeholder={placeholder}
          placeholderTextColor={theme.colors.lilac_40}
          autoCapitalize={autoCapitalize}
          autoCorrect={false}
          underlineColorAndroid="transparent"
          value={value}
          onChangeText={onChange}
          maxLength={maxLength}
          borderBottomWidth={1}
          secureTextEntry={secure}
          onFocus={() => handleFocus(true)}
          onBlur={() => handleFocus(false)}
          keyboardType={keyboardType}
          returnKeyType={returnKeyType}
          style={styles.inputStyles}
          {...restProps}
        />
        <OpenKeyboard inputRef={ref} />
      </Animated.View>
    );
  },
);

InputComponent.displayName = 'InputComponent';

export default InputComponent;

import 'react-native-gesture-handler'; // Important that react-native-gesture-handler is the first import on top!
import { useReactiveVar } from '@apollo/client';
import {
  DefaultTheme as NavigationTheme,
  NavigationContainer,
  useNavigationContainerRef,
} from '@react-navigation/native';
import { createStackNavigator } from '@react-navigation/stack';
import { ThemeProvider } from 'emotion-theming';
import { useFonts } from 'expo-font';
import * as React from 'react';
import { analytics } from './lib/analytics';
import {
  authenticationState,
  AUTHENTICATION_ENUM,
} from './authenticationState';
import ToastProvider from './components/Toast';
import theme, { colors } from './lib/rainbow/theme';
import SWRProvider from './lib/swr';
import BottomTabNavigator from './navigation/BottomTabNavigator';
import DefaultErrorScreen from './screens/HomeStack/HomeScreen/DefaultErrorScreen';
import EnterOtpScreen from './screens/RootStack/EnterOtpScreen/EnterOtpScreen';
import OtpLoginScreen from './screens/RootStack/OtpLoginScreen/OtpLoginScreen';
import PasswordLoginScreen from './screens/RootStack/PasswordLoginScreen/PasswordLoginScreen';
import SplashScreen from './screens/RootStack/SplashScreen/SplashScreen';
import attemptToRefreshToken from './session/attemptToRefreshToken';
import { AddressProvider } from './store/AddressContext';
import { PushNotificationsProvider } from './store/PushNotificationContext';

const RootStack = createStackNavigator();

export default function AuthenticaticationRouter() {
  const navigationRef = useNavigationContainerRef();
  const routeNameRef = React.useRef();
  const [initialPageViewLogged, setInitialPageViewLogged] =
    React.useState(false);

  const [fontsLoaded] = useFonts({
    'Poppins-Regular': require('../assets/fonts/Poppins-Regular.ttf'), //eslint-disable-line
    'Poppins-Medium': require('../assets/fonts/Poppins-Medium.ttf'), //eslint-disable-line
    'Poppins-SemiBold': require('../assets/fonts/Poppins-SemiBold.ttf'), //eslint-disable-line
    'Poppins-Light': require('../assets/fonts/Poppins-Light.ttf'), //eslint-disable-line
    'Poppins-Bold': require('../assets/fonts/Poppins-Bold.ttf'), //eslint-disable-line
  });

  const navigationTheme = {
    ...NavigationTheme,
    colors: {
      ...NavigationTheme.colors,
      primary: colors.royalBlue,
      background: 'white',
    },
  };

  const currentAuthenticationState = useReactiveVar(authenticationState);

  const logScreenTransitionToAnalytics = React.useCallback(async () => {
    const previousRouteName = routeNameRef.current;
    const currentRouteName = navigationRef.getCurrentRoute().name;

    if (previousRouteName !== currentRouteName && analytics) {
      // The line below uses React Native Firebase tracker
      // https://rnfirebase.io/reference/analytics#logScreenView
      await analytics.logScreenView({
        screen_name: currentRouteName,
      });
    }
    // Save the current route name for later comparison
    routeNameRef.current = currentRouteName;
  }, [routeNameRef, navigationRef]);

  React.useEffect(() => {
    async function logInitialPageView() {
      if (
        !initialPageViewLogged &&
        currentAuthenticationState !== AUTHENTICATION_ENUM.PENDING &&
        analytics
      ) {
        if (currentAuthenticationState === AUTHENTICATION_ENUM.LOGGED_IN) {
          await analytics.logScreenView({
            screen_name: 'HomeScreen',
          });
        } else {
          await analytics.logScreenView({
            screen_name: 'LoginScreen',
          });
        }
        setInitialPageViewLogged(true);
      }
    }
    logInitialPageView();
  }, [currentAuthenticationState, initialPageViewLogged]);

  if (
    !fontsLoaded ||
    currentAuthenticationState === AUTHENTICATION_ENUM.PENDING
  ) {
    return <SplashScreen />;
  }

  const isLoggedIn =
    currentAuthenticationState === AUTHENTICATION_ENUM.LOGGED_IN;

  const isServiceUnavailable =
    currentAuthenticationState === AUTHENTICATION_ENUM.SERVICE_UNAVAILABLE;

  const isNetworkUnavailable =
    currentAuthenticationState === AUTHENTICATION_ENUM.NO_NETWORK;

  return (
    <SWRProvider>
      <ThemeProvider theme={theme}>
        <NavigationContainer
          ref={navigationRef}
          theme={navigationTheme}
          onReady={() => {
            routeNameRef.current = navigationRef.getCurrentRoute().name;
          }}
          onStateChange={logScreenTransitionToAnalytics}>
          <ToastProvider>
            <RootStack.Navigator screenOptions={{ headerShown: false }}>
              {(isServiceUnavailable || isNetworkUnavailable) && (
                <RootStack.Screen name="DefaultErrorScreen">
                  {() => (
                    <DefaultErrorScreen
                      onRetry={attemptToRefreshToken('DefaultErrorScreen')}
                      showLogOutButton
                    />
                  )}
                </RootStack.Screen>
              )}
              {!isLoggedIn ? (
                <>
                  <RootStack.Screen
                    name="LoginScreen"
                    component={OtpLoginScreen}
                    options={{
                      animationTypeForReplace: isLoggedIn ? 'pop' : 'push',
                    }}
                  />
                  <RootStack.Screen
                    name="EnterOtpScreen"
                    component={EnterOtpScreen}
                  />
                  <RootStack.Screen
                    name="PasswordLoginScreen"
                    component={PasswordLoginScreen}
                    options={{
                      animationTypeForReplace: isLoggedIn ? 'pop' : 'push',
                    }}
                  />
                </>
              ) : (
                <RootStack.Screen name="BottomTabNavigator">
                  {(props) => (
                    <AddressProvider>
                      <PushNotificationsProvider>
                        <BottomTabNavigator {...props} />
                      </PushNotificationsProvider>
                    </AddressProvider>
                  )}
                </RootStack.Screen>
              )}
            </RootStack.Navigator>
          </ToastProvider>
        </NavigationContainer>
      </ThemeProvider>
    </SWRProvider>
  );
}

# ScrollView

Component that wraps platform ScrollView while providing integration with touch locking "responder" system.

Keep in mind that ScrollViews must have a bounded height in order to work, since they contain unbounded-height children into a bounded container (via a scroll interaction). In order to bound the height of a ScrollView, either set the height of the view directly (discouraged) or make sure all parent views have bounded height. Forgetting to transfer `{flex: 1}` down the view stack can lead to errors here, which the element inspector makes quick to debug.

Doesn't yet support other contained responders from blocking this scroll view from becoming the responder.

`<ScrollView>` vs `<FlatList>` - which one to use?

`ScrollView` renders all its react child components at once, but this has a performance downside.

Imagine you have a very long list of items you want to display, maybe several screens worth of content. Creating JS components and native views for everything all at once, much of which may not even be shown, will contribute to slow rendering and increased memory usage.

This is where `FlatList` comes into play. `FlatList` renders items lazily, when they are about to appear, and removes items that scroll way off screen to save memory and processing time.

`FlatList` is also handy if you want to render separators between your items, multiple columns, infinite scroll loading, or any number of other features it supports out of the box.

### View PropsŌĆŗ

Inherits View Props.

### `StickyHeaderComponent`ŌĆŗ

A React Component that will be used to render sticky headers, should be used together with `stickyHeaderIndices`. You may need to set this component if your sticky header uses custom transforms, for example, when you want your list to have an animated and hidable header. If a component has not been provided, the default `ScrollViewStickyHeader` component will be used.

| Type |
| --- |
| component, element |

### `alwaysBounceHorizontal` iOSŌĆŗ

When true, the scroll view bounces horizontally when it reaches the end even if the content is smaller than the scroll view itself.

| Type | Default |
| --- | --- |
| bool | `true` when `horizontal={true}``false` otherwise |

### `alwaysBounceVertical` iOSŌĆŗ

When true, the scroll view bounces vertically when it reaches the end even if the content is smaller than the scroll view itself.

| Type | Default |
| --- | --- |
| bool | `false` when `vertical={true}``true` otherwise |

### `automaticallyAdjustContentInsets` iOSŌĆŗ

Controls whether iOS should automatically adjust the content inset for scroll views that are placed behind a navigation bar or tab bar/toolbar.

| Type | Default |
| --- | --- |
| bool | `true` |

### `automaticallyAdjustKeyboardInsets` iOSŌĆŗ

Controls whether the ScrollView should automatically adjust its `contentInset` and `scrollViewInsets` when the Keyboard changes its size.

| Type | Default |
| --- | --- |
| bool | `false` |

### `automaticallyAdjustsScrollIndicatorInsets` iOSŌĆŗ

Controls whether iOS should automatically adjust the scroll indicator insets. See Apple's documentation on the property.

| Type | Default |
| --- | --- |
| bool | `true` |

### `bounces` iOSŌĆŗ

When true, the scroll view bounces when it reaches the end of the content if the content is larger than the scroll view along the axis of the scroll direction. When `false`, it disables all bouncing even if the `alwaysBounce*` props are `true`.

| Type | Default |
| --- | --- |
| bool | `true` |

### `bouncesZoom` iOSŌĆŗ

When `true`, gestures can drive zoom past min/max and the zoom will animate to the min/max value at gesture end, otherwise the zoom will not exceed the limits.

| Type | Default |
| --- | --- |
| bool | `true` |

### `canCancelContentTouches` iOSŌĆŗ

When `false`, once tracking starts, won't try to drag if the touch moves.

| Type | Default |
| --- | --- |
| bool | `true` |

### `centerContent` iOSŌĆŗ

When `true`, the scroll view automatically centers the content when the content is smaller than the scroll view bounds; when the content is larger than the scroll view, this property has no effect.

| Type | Default |
| --- | --- |
| bool | `false` |

### `contentContainerStyle`ŌĆŗ

These styles will be applied to the scroll view content container which wraps all of the child views. Example:

```
return (  <ScrollView contentContainerStyle={styles.contentContainer}>  </ScrollView>);...const styles = StyleSheet.create({  contentContainer: {    paddingVertical: 20  }});
```

| Type |
| --- |
| View Style |

### `contentInset` iOSŌĆŗ

The amount by which the scroll view content is inset from the edges of the scroll view.

| Type | Default |
| --- | --- |
| object: `{top: number, left: number, bottom: number, right: number}` | `{top: 0, left: 0, bottom: 0, right: 0}` |

### `contentInsetAdjustmentBehavior` iOSŌĆŗ

This property specifies how the safe area insets are used to modify the content area of the scroll view. Available on iOS 11 and later.

| Type | Default |
| --- | --- |
| enum(`'automatic'`, `'scrollableAxes'`, `'never'`, `'always'`) | `'never'` |

### `contentOffset`ŌĆŗ

Used to manually set the starting scroll offset.

| Type | Default |
| --- | --- |
| Point | `{x: 0, y: 0}` |

### `decelerationRate`ŌĆŗ

A floating-point number that determines how quickly the scroll view decelerates after the user lifts their finger. You may also use string shortcuts `"normal"` and `"fast"` which match the underlying iOS settings for `UIScrollViewDecelerationRateNormal` and `UIScrollViewDecelerationRateFast` respectively.
- `'normal'` 0.998 on iOS, 0.985 on Android.
- `'fast'`, 0.99 on iOS, 0.9 on Android.

| Type | Default |
| --- | --- |
| enum(`'fast'`, `'normal'`), number | `'normal'` |

### `directionalLockEnabled` iOSŌĆŗ

When true, the ScrollView will try to lock to only vertical or horizontal scrolling while dragging.

| Type | Default |
| --- | --- |
| bool | `false` |

### `disableIntervalMomentum`ŌĆŗ

When true, the scroll view stops on the next index (in relation to scroll position at release) regardless of how fast the gesture is. This can be used for pagination when the page is less than the width of the horizontal ScrollView or the height of the vertical ScrollView.

| Type | Default |
| --- | --- |
| bool | `false` |

### `disableScrollViewPanResponder`ŌĆŗ

When true, the default JS pan responder on the ScrollView is disabled, and full control over touches inside the ScrollView is left to its child components. This is particularly useful if `snapToInterval` is enabled, since it does not follow typical touch patterns. Do not use this on regular ScrollView use cases without `snapToInterval` as it may cause unexpected touches to occur while scrolling.

| Type | Default |
| --- | --- |
| bool | `false` |

### `endFillColor` AndroidŌĆŗ

Sometimes a scrollview takes up more space than its content fills. When this is the case, this prop will fill the rest of the scrollview with a color to avoid setting a background and creating unnecessary overdraw. This is an advanced optimization that is not needed in the general case.

| Type |
| --- |
| color |

### `fadingEdgeLength` AndroidŌĆŗ

Fades out the edges of the the scroll content.

If the value is greater than `0`, the fading edges will be set accordingly to the current scroll direction and position, indicating if there is more content to show.

| Type | Default |
| --- | --- |
| number | `0` |

### `horizontal`ŌĆŗ

When `true`, the scroll view's children are arranged horizontally in a row instead of vertically in a column.

| Type | Default |
| --- | --- |
| bool | `false` |

### `indicatorStyle` iOSŌĆŗ

The style of the scroll indicators.
- `'default'` same as `black`.
- `'black'`, scroll indicator is `black`. This style is good against a light background.
- `'white'`, scroll indicator is `white`. This style is good against a dark background.

| Type | Default |
| --- | --- |
| enum(`'default'`, `'black'`, `'white'`) | `'default'` |

### `invertStickyHeaders`ŌĆŗ

If sticky headers should stick at the bottom instead of the top of the ScrollView. This is usually used with inverted ScrollViews.

| Type | Default |
| --- | --- |
| bool | `false` |

### `keyboardDismissMode`ŌĆŗ

Determines whether the keyboard gets dismissed in response to a drag.
- `'none'`, drags do not dismiss the keyboard.
- `'on-drag'`, the keyboard is dismissed when a drag begins.

**iOS Only**
- `'interactive'`, the keyboard is dismissed interactively with the drag and moves in synchrony with the touch, dragging upwards cancels the dismissal. On Android this is not supported and it will have the same behavior as `'none'`.

| Type | Default |
| --- | --- |
| enum(`'none'`, `'on-drag'`) Androidenum(`'none'`, `'on-drag'`, `'interactive'`) iOS | `'none'` |

### `keyboardShouldPersistTaps`ŌĆŗ

Determines when the keyboard should stay visible after a tap.
- `'never'` tapping outside of the focused text input when the keyboard is up dismisses the keyboard. When this happens, children won't receive the tap.
- `'always'`, the keyboard will not dismiss automatically, and the scroll view will not catch taps, but children of the scroll view can catch taps.
- `'handled'`, the keyboard will not dismiss automatically when the tap was handled by children of the scroll view (or captured by an ancestor).
- `false`, **_deprecated_**, use `'never'` instead
- `true`, **_deprecated_**, use `'always'` instead

### `maintainVisibleContentPosition`ŌĆŗ

When set, the scroll view will adjust the scroll position so that the first child that is currently visible and at or beyond `minIndexForVisible` will not change position. This is useful for lists that are loading content in both directions, e.g. a chat thread, where new messages coming in might otherwise cause the scroll position to jump. A value of 0 is common, but other values such as 1 can be used to skip loading spinners or other content that should not maintain position.

The optional `autoscrollToTopThreshold` can be used to make the content automatically scroll to the top after making the adjustment if the user was within the threshold of the top before the adjustment was made. This is also useful for chat-like applications where you want to see new messages scroll into place, but not if the user has scrolled up a ways and it would be disruptive to scroll a bunch.

Caveat 1: Reordering elements in the scrollview with this enabled will probably cause jumpiness and jank. It can be fixed, but there are currently no plans to do so. For now, don't re-order the content of any ScrollViews or Lists that use this feature.

Caveat 2: This uses `contentOffset` and `frame.origin` in native code to compute visibility. Occlusion, transforms, and other complexity won't be taken into account as to whether content is "visible" or not.

Scrolls to a given x, y offset, either immediately, with a smooth animation.

**Example:**

Note: The weird function signature is due to the fact that, for historical reasons, the function also accepts separate arguments as an alternative to the options object. This is deprecated due to ambiguity (y before x), and SHOULD NOT BE USED.

If this is a vertical ScrollView scrolls to the bottom. If this is a horizontal ScrollView scrolls to the right.

Use `scrollToEnd({animated: true})` for smooth animated scrolling, `scrollToEnd({animated: false})` for immediate scrolling. If no options are passed, `animated` defaults to `true`.Edit this pageLast updated on Aug 19, 2024

And I wonder - have we configured something incorrectly for the input fields in this view?
Based on the information you've provided and the issue you're experiencing with the keyboard not showing up after tapping input fields on the login screen, it seems that the `keyboardShouldPersistTaps` prop might be missing or incorrectly set on the ScrollView or any parent scrollable component wrapping your input fields.

Let's review the `PasswordLoginScreen` component:

1. First, make sure that the input fields are wrapped in a ScrollView or KeyboardAvoidingView. It appears you're already using KeyboardAvoidingView, which is good.

2. Add the `keyboardShouldPersistTaps` prop to the KeyboardAvoidingView:

```jsx
<KeyboardAvoidingView
  flex={1}
  mx="8"
  behavior={Platform.OS === 'ios' ? 'padding' : undefined}
  keyboardVerticalOffset={50}
  keyboardShouldPersistTaps="handled"
  enabled>
  {/* ... input fields ... */}
</KeyboardAvoidingView>
```

3. If you're using a ScrollView inside the KeyboardAvoidingView, add the prop there as well:

```jsx
<ScrollView keyboardShouldPersistTaps="handled">
  {/* ... input fields ... */}
</ScrollView>
```

4. Ensure that the InputComponent is correctly handling focus. In the InputComponent, make sure the TextInput has the proper props:

```jsx
<TextInput
  ref={ref}
  // ... other props ...
  onFocus={() => handleFocus(true)}
  onBlur={() => handleFocus(false)}
/>
```

5. In the PasswordLoginScreen, make sure you're correctly passing and using the ref:

```jsx
const emailInputRef = React.useRef(null);
const passwordInputRef = React.useRef(null);

// ... in the render ...

<InputComponent
  ref={emailInputRef}
  // ... other props ...
/>

<InputComponent
  ref={passwordInputRef}
  // ... other props ...
/>
```

6. Additionally, ensure that no parent components or overlays are accidentally capturing or blocking touch events that should reach the input fields.

7. If the issue persists, you might want to add some logging or debugging to ensure that the onFocus handlers are being called when you tap the input fields.

If after implementing these suggestions the problem still occurs, there might be an issue with how the navigation or the overall app structure is handling keyboard events. In that case, you might need to investigate at a higher level in your app's component hierarchy.

After (finally) FaceTiming with two customers that was willing to share

The issue stems from the way we wrapped the KeyboardAvoidingView in a
ScrollView. However, we were unable to reproduce this for two reasons:

2. It only affected users where `PixelRatio.getFontScale() > 1`

with increased font size scroll the page. However, we found reports that
this component nesting froze the keyboard on production builds[2].

[1] 70e6ed5

Removing the outer ScrollView fixed the issue. However, this means that

Certainly! I'll update the PR description to reflect the findings and solutions from your commit message. Here's a revised version of the PR description:

```markdown
## What

This PR fixes a critical issue where users were unable to use the keyboard when attempting to log in. We've also added analytics to track keyboard interaction attempts.

### The Real Problem

After direct observation with affected customers, we discovered:

1. The keyboard was appearing, but the keys were unresponsive.
2. This issue only occurred in production builds, not in preview builds.
3. It specifically affected users with `PixelRatio.getFontScale() > 1`.

The root cause was our implementation of `KeyboardAvoidingView` and `ScrollView`. In `Page.js`, we conditionally added a `ScrollView` outside of the `KeyboardAvoidingView` for users with increased font size. This component nesting caused keyboard freezing in production builds (see [react-native#36859](https://github.com/facebook/react-native/issues/36859)).

## Why

We received multiple reports of keyboard issues during login. This fix ensures all users can successfully interact with the login form, regardless of their device's font scale settings.

## How

1. Removed the outer `ScrollView` in `Page.js`.
2. Refactored the layout:
   - Condensed top spacing to bring input fields higher.
   - Added a `ScrollView` inside the `KeyboardAvoidingView`.
   - Replaced the bottom banner with a link for alternative login methods below the main CTA.

3. Retained the "show keyboard" icon and Firebase analytics for additional user assistance and issue tracking.

These changes solve the keyboard freeze while maintaining accessibility for users with extreme font size settings.

## Additional Notes

- The initially proposed "show keyboard" button wouldn't have solved the core issue, but we've kept it as a fallback option.
- Firebase analytics will help us monitor any ongoing keyboard interaction issues.

Part of DEL-2659
```

```
Ō×£ ffmpeg -i first.mp4 -c:v libx264 -crf 18  -c:a copy -vf scale=443:960  first-out.mp4

...

Press [q] to stop, [?] for help

    Last message repeated 1 times
[swscaler @ 0x148128000] deprecated pixel format used, make sure you did set range correctly
[swscaler @ 0x121550000] deprecated pixel format used, make sure you did set range correctly
[libx264 @ 0x137807210] width not divisible by 2 (443x960)
[vost#0:0/libx264 @ 0x1378078e0] Error while opening encoder - maybe incorrect parameters such as bit_rate, rate, width or height.
[vf#0:0 @ 0x6000028cdb00] Error sending frames to consumers: Generic error in an external library
[vf#0:0 @ 0x6000028cdb00] Task finished with error code: -542398533 (Generic error in an external library)
[vf#0:0 @ 0x6000028cdb00] Terminating thread with return code -542398533 (Generic error in an external library)
[vost#0:0/libx264 @ 0x1378078e0] Could not open encoder before EOF
[vost#0:0/libx264 @ 0x1378078e0] Task finished with error code: -22 (Invalid argument)
[vost#0:0/libx264 @ 0x1378078e0] Terminating thread with return code -22 (Invalid argument)
[out#0/mp4 @ 0x600002fcc0c0] Nothing was written into output file, because at least one of its streams received no packets.
frame=    0 fps=0.0 q=0.0 Lsize=       0KiB time=N/A bitrate=N/A speed=N/A
Conversion failed!
```
The error message indicates a few issues:

1. The main problem is that the width you've specified (443) is not divisible by 2. H.264 encoding requires even dimensions.

2. There's a warning about deprecated pixel format, which might be related to the input video's color space.

To fix these issues, try the following:

1. Make sure the width is even. You can either round it up to 444 or down to 442:

```
ffmpeg -i first.mp4 -c:v libx264 -crf 18 -c:a copy -vf scale=444:960 first-out.mp4
```

2. If you still get the pixel format warning, you can try specifying the pixel format explicitly:

```
ffmpeg -i first.mp4 -c:v libx264 -crf 18 -c:a copy -vf scale=444:960,format=yuv420p first-out.mp4
```

3. If you want to maintain the aspect ratio and just ensure the width is even, you can use:

```
ffmpeg -i first.mp4 -c:v libx264 -crf 18 -c:a copy -vf "scale=trunc(oh*a/2)*2:960" first-out.mp4
```

This will calculate the width based on the input aspect ratio and ensure it's even.

4. If you're trying to fit the video within certain dimensions while maintaining the aspect ratio, you can use:

```
ffmpeg -i first.mp4 -c:v libx264 -crf 18 -c:a copy -vf "scale=444:960:force_original_aspect_ratio=decrease,pad=444:960:(ow-iw)/2:(oh-ih)/2" first-out.mp4
```

This will scale the video to fit within 444x960, maintaining the aspect ratio, and pad with black if necessary.
